_comment: Metatile legend for LAYOUT_LAEKON_ROUTE1 (Tidal Reach coastal route east of Zeegem). Primary tileset gTileset_General, secondary gTileset_Dewford (same pair as LAYOUT_ZEEGEM). Every entry below was copied from a real, working map already in this repo -- IDs, collision, and elevation are not invented. Sources: data/layouts/Zeegem/map.bin (shoreline + rocks + solo trees + tree wall), data/layouts/Route101/map.bin (tall grass, ledge, plain grass), data/layouts/Route104/map.bin (verified vanilla water collision/elevation convention). collision: 0 = walkable, 1 = blocked (per pokeemerald map.bin bit 10-11 encoding). elevation: bits 12-15, 3 matches land, 1 matches water -- SEE THE 'why elevation 1' NOTE in shore_and_sea below, this was the source of a real in-game bug (water was walkable) caused by an earlier version of this legend wrongly recording elevation 0 for water.

CLEARING.source: Zeegem main plaza ground (SandPit_Center graphic), reused as rest-spot clearing accent

trees_and_rocks._comment: Verified against Zeegem's real forest art (not just behavior bytes): 243 is a dense INTERIOR canopy tile with no visible trunk, meant to be surrounded by more forest on multiple sides. 23a and 239 both render a visible trunk/mound and are meant ONLY for a genuine forest-edge-to-open-ground transition (23a) or a deliberate standalone tree sitting alone in a field (239) -- never as filler inside a solid wall. Mixing them into a wall creates a ragged trunk-poking-out look. Rule: a sealing wall is 100% one tile (243), no exceptions.

TREE_WALL.source: Zeegem north/west border fill -- the ONLY tile used for sealing walls

TREE_EDGE.source: Zeegem's diagonal forest-to-sand boundary -- use only where forest genuinely borders open ground on one side, never inside a wall

TREE_SOLO.source: Zeegem standalone landmark tree in open sand -- use only for deliberate scattered decoration, never inside a wall

ledge_jump_south._comment: One-way hop south. Verbatim 5-wide unit from Route101 rows 6-7 (06e cap / 087 x3 hop tiles / 0d6 cap). Extend the middle run for a wider ledge; caps stay single-width at each end.

shore_and_sea._comment: Two-level coastline copied tile-for-tile from Zeegem's own south shore. Baseline coastline: land / SHORE_FILL row / SEA rows. A cove is formed by shifting the SHORE_FILL row one row further north for a column span, bridged by the corner pair below.
shore_and_sea.why elevation 1: Water traversability is NOT decided by the collision bit here -- collision is 0 (unblocked) on every water tile, same as vanilla. The actual blocker is elevation mismatch: src/event_object_movement.c's IsElevationMismatchAt() compares the player's current elevation to the tile's elevation and blocks on any difference, EXCEPT it treats elevation 0 (ELEVATION_TRANSITION) and 15 (ELEVATION_MULTI_LEVEL) as wildcards that never mismatch (include/global.fieldmap.h). Land here is elevation 3, so water must be a real, distinct, non-wildcard elevation for the mismatch (and therefore the Surf prompt) to trigger at all. An earlier version of this legend recorded water as elevation 0, which is the ELEVATION_TRANSITION wildcard -- it silently matched every possible player elevation, so nothing ever blocked walking onto water. Verified correct value by reading data/layouts/Route104/map.bin (vanilla): water is overwhelmingly (collision 0, elevation 1) there; land/tall grass/sand are (collision 0, elevation 3). Zeegem's own hand-authored water was already elevation 1 all along -- only this generated legend had the bug.

SHORE_CORNER_L.note: west end of a shore-edge run; land sits to its west and north-west

SHORE_FILL.note: straight shore-edge run, tiles freely with itself

SHORE_CORNER_R.note: east end of a shore-edge run; land sits to its east and north-east

SHORE_STEP_L.note: sits directly under SHORE_CORNER_L, bridges edge row down to open sea

SEA.note: open deep water, tiles freely with itself in all directions

border_fill._comment: border.bin (repeats infinitely beyond the map edges). Zeegem's own border.bin is 4x SEA (0x170) since its south edge is coastline -- Route1 reuses the same so the horizon beyond the sea reads as more open water.
